nodes.name: Academy Awards
nodes.description: Oscar-winning films and landmark moments in Academy history

items.prompt: Which actress holds the all-time record with four Best Actress wins, for *Morning Glory* (1933), *Guess Who's Coming to Dinner* (1967), *The Lion in Winter* (1968), and *On Golden Pond* (1981)?
items.explanation:
Won Best Actress four times — a record no other actor has matched in any acting category
Her 1968 win for *The Lion in Winter* was a tie with Barbra Streisand for *Funny Girl* — the only tied Best Actress result in Oscar history
Received 12 acting nominations spanning 48 years (1933–1981)
Fiercely independent; defied Hollywood studio expectations and rarely attended award ceremonies
Her fourth win came at age 74 for *On Golden Pond*, opposite Henry Fonda in his final film

items.prompt: Which British-Irish method actor is the only person to win Best Actor three times, for *My Left Foot* (1989), *There Will Be Blood* (2007), and *Lincoln* (2012)?
items.explanation:
Only actor to win three Best Actor Oscars — a record in the lead acting category
Extreme method acting: stayed in a wheelchair on set for *My Left Foot*; learned Czech for *The Unbearable Lightness of Being*; refused to break character during *There Will Be Blood*
Son of Poet Laureate Cecil Day-Lewis; holds dual British-Irish citizenship
Announced retirement from acting in 2017 after *Phantom Thread*
Trained as a cobbler during one of his extended acting hiatuses in the 1990s

items.prompt: Which character actor holds the record for most Best Supporting Actor wins with three, for *Come and Get It* (1936), *Kentucky* (1938), and *The Westerner* (1940)?
items.explanation:
Won three of the first five Best Supporting Actor Oscars ever awarded (the category began in 1936)
Record three Supporting Actor wins has never been matched
Known for playing grizzled old-timers despite being only in his 40s during his winning streak
Appeared in over 230 films across a career spanning the 1920s–1970s
Later starred in the TV series *The Real McCoys* (1957–1963), one of the first rural sitcoms

items.prompt: Which actress holds the record for most acting Oscar nominations (21), winning three times for *Kramer vs. Kramer* (1979), *Sophie's Choice* (1982), and *The Iron Lady* (2011)?
items.explanation:
21 acting nominations — far more than any other performer in Oscar history
Three wins: Best Supporting Actress (*Kramer vs. Kramer*), Best Actress (*Sophie's Choice*, *The Iron Lady*)
Mastered German, Polish, Italian, and Danish for various roles; accent work is considered unmatched
First nominated in 1978 for *The Deer Hunter*; spanned five decades of nominations
Once told by a Universal executive she was "not pretty enough" for a role; proved otherwise across a 50-year career

items.prompt: Which actor won three Oscars — Best Actor for *One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest* (1975) and *As Good as It Gets* (1997), and Best Supporting Actor for *Terms of Endearment* (1983) — and received 12 total nominations?
items.explanation:
12 Oscar nominations — the most for any male actor in history
Three wins across two categories: two Best Actor, one Best Supporting Actor
One of several men to win both lead and supporting acting Oscars, along with Robert De Niro, Gene Hackman, Jack Lemmon, and Kevin Spacey
Iconic roles include Jack Torrance (*The Shining*), the Joker (*Batman*, 1989), and Col. Jessup (*A Few Good Men*)
Notoriously missed out on several roles: was considered for Michael Corleone in *The Godfather* before Al Pacino was cast

links.text: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest

items.prompt: Which British actress won Best Actress twice, as Scarlett O'Hara in *Gone with the Wind* (1939) and as Blanche DuBois in *A Streetcar Named Desire* (1951)?
items.explanation:
Won two of only four roles she played in Hollywood — an extraordinary hit rate
Beat 1,400 candidates for the role of Scarlett O'Hara in one of Hollywood's most publicized casting searches
*A Streetcar Named Desire* also starred Marlon Brando; she reprised the role she had played on London's West End under Laurence Olivier's direction
Married to Laurence Olivier from 1940 to 1960; their relationship was among the most publicized of the era
Suffered from bipolar disorder and tuberculosis; died at age 53 in 1967
items.cardFront: 2 Best Actress wins: Scarlett O'Hara, Blanche DuBois

items.prompt: Which actor won back-to-back Best Actor Oscars for *Philadelphia* (1993) and *Forrest Gump* (1994), only the second actor to achieve consecutive wins?
items.explanation:
Back-to-back Best Actor wins in 1994 and 1995 — only the second actor to do so, after Spencer Tracy (1937–1938)
*Philadelphia*: played Andrew Beckett, one of the first major Hollywood portrayals of AIDS discrimination
*Forrest Gump*: the film pioneered digital compositing to insert the character into historical footage
Also acclaimed for *Cast Away* (2000), *Saving Private Ryan* (1998), and voicing Woody in the *Toy Story* franchise
Produced the HBO miniseries *Band of Brothers* (2001) and *The Pacific* (2010)

items.prompt: Which actor was the first to win back-to-back Best Actor Oscars, for *Captains Courageous* (1937) and *Boys Town* (1938)?
items.explanation:
First actor to win consecutive Best Actor Oscars (1937–1938); Tom Hanks later matched the feat in 1994–1995
Received nine Best Actor nominations total — a record that stood for decades
Partnered with Katharine Hepburn in nine films between 1942 and 1967; their off-screen relationship was an open secret in Hollywood
Final film was *Guess Who's Coming to Dinner* (1967); died 17 days after filming ended
His Best Actor statue for *Boys Town* was inscribed "To Dick" (his character's name) at his request

items.prompt: Which actor won Best Actor for *On the Waterfront* (1954) and *The Godfather* (1972), but refused his second Oscar, sending Sacheen Littlefeather to decline it?
items.explanation:
Refused his 1973 Best Actor Oscar for *The Godfather*; sent Sacheen Littlefeather to the podium to protest Hollywood's treatment of Native Americans
His performance in *On the Waterfront* produced the iconic line "I coulda been a contender"
Pioneered method acting in American cinema, studied under Stella Adler
Insisted on improvisation; stuffed his cheeks with cotton (later a dental appliance) to create Vito Corleone's distinctive voice
Also starred in *A Streetcar Named Desire* (1951), *Apocalypse Now* (1979), and *Last Tango in Paris* (1972)
items.cardFront: 2 Best Actor wins; refused Oscar for *The Godfather*

items.prompt: Which actress won Best Actress for both *The Accused* (1988) and *The Silence of the Lambs* (1991), and later directed several films?
items.explanation:
Won Best Actress three ceremonies apart: *The Accused* at the 61st ceremony (1989) and *The Silence of the Lambs* at the 64th ceremony (1992)
*The Silence of the Lambs* swept the Big Five — only the third film to do so, after *It Happened One Night* and *One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest*
Child star from age three; first major role in Scorsese's *Taxi Driver* (1976) at age 13
Graduated magna cum laude from Yale University in 1985
Directed films including *Little Man Tate* (1991) and *Money Monster* (2016)
items.cardFront: 2 Best Actress wins: *The Accused*, *Silence of the Lambs*

items.prompt: Which actor won Best Supporting Actor for *Glory* (1989) and Best Actor for *Training Day* (2001), becoming only the second African American to win lead actor?
items.explanation:
Won in both supporting and lead categories — one of the few actors to achieve this
Second African American to win Best Actor (after Sidney Poitier in 1964), with a 38-year gap between the two wins
*Glory*: played Private Trip, a formerly enslaved Union soldier in the Civil War
*Training Day*: played corrupt LAPD detective Alonzo Harris in a departure from his typically heroic roles
Received nine Oscar nominations total across a career spanning 1987–2021

items.prompt: Which actress won three Best Actress Oscars, for *Fargo* (1996), *Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri* (2017), and *Nomadland* (2020)?
items.explanation:
Three Best Actress wins ties her with Ingrid Bergman for second-most female acting Oscars (behind Katharine Hepburn's four)
Married to Coen brothers collaborator Joel Coen; *Fargo* was directed by the Coen brothers
*Nomadland*: also served as a producer, winning Best Picture as well as Best Actress
During her 2018 acceptance speech for *Three Billboards*, she introduced the concept of an "inclusion rider" to mainstream awareness
Known for minimal red-carpet preparation; once famously said she would never get cosmetic surgery

items.prompt: Which Welsh actor became the oldest Best Actor winner at age 83 for *The Father* (2020), decades after his first win as Hannibal Lecter in *The Silence of the Lambs*?
items.explanation:
Oldest Best Actor winner at 83 years old for *The Father* (2020), surpassing Henry Fonda who won at 76
First win was for Hannibal Lecter in *The Silence of the Lambs* (1991) — a role with only about 16 minutes of screen time
His *Silence of the Lambs* performance is one of the shortest screen times ever to win a lead acting Oscar
Born in Port Talbot, Wales; became a U.S. citizen in 2000
Was not present at the 2021 ceremony; his *Father* win over Chadwick Boseman (posthumous favorite) was one of the most debated Oscar results in recent history
items.cardFront: Oldest Best Actor at 83; Hannibal Lecter

links.text: Oldest Best Actor at 83; Hannibal Lecter

items.prompt: Which actress became the youngest competitive Oscar winner at age 10, winning Best Supporting Actress for *Paper Moon* (1973) opposite her real-life father?
items.explanation:
Won Best Supporting Actress at age 10 at the 46th Academy Awards (April 1974) — still the youngest competitive winner ever
Played Addie Loggins, a con artist's daughter, opposite her father Ryan O'Neal in *Paper Moon*
Directed by Peter Bogdanovich; shot in black and white to evoke Depression-era Kansas
Her career declined after early success; she later wrote a memoir, *A Paper Life* (2004), about her turbulent childhood
Shirley Temple received an honorary juvenile Oscar at age 6 in 1935, but that was non-competitive

items.prompt: Which actress became the first African American to win an Oscar, taking Best Supporting Actress for her role as Mammy in *Gone with the Wind* (1939)?
items.explanation:
First African American to win an Academy Award, at the 12th ceremony (February 29, 1940)
Won for playing Mammy in *Gone with the Wind*; the role has been both celebrated and criticized
Seated at a segregated table at the side of the Cocoanut Grove nightclub during the ceremony, away from the film's white cast
Also the first Black woman to sing on American radio
Appeared in over 300 films, often in uncredited maid roles; responded to criticism by saying she'd "rather play a maid than be one"

items.prompt: Which actor became the first African American to win Best Actor, for *Lilies of the Field* (1963), and later received an honorary Oscar for his overall contribution to cinema?
items.explanation:
First African American to win Best Actor at the 36th Academy Awards (April 1964)
Won for *Lilies of the Field*, playing Homer Smith, an itinerant handyman who helps German-speaking nuns build a chapel in the Arizona desert
1967 was his landmark year: starred in *In the Heat of the Night*, *Guess Who's Coming to Dinner*, and *To Sir, with Love*
Received an Honorary Oscar in 2002 for "extraordinary performances and unique presence on the screen"
Born in Miami, raised in the Bahamas; largely self-taught as an actor after being rejected by the American Negro Theatre

items.prompt: Which Swedish actress won three Oscars — Best Actress for *Gaslight* (1944) and *Anastasia* (1956), and Best Supporting Actress for *Murder on the Orient Express* (1974)?
items.explanation:
Three Oscar wins across two acting categories: two Best Actress, one Best Supporting Actress
Also starred in *Casablanca* (1942), *Notorious* (1946), and *Autumn Sonata* (1978, directed by Ingmar Bergman — no relation)
Her affair with Italian director Roberto Rossellini in 1950 caused a scandal that effectively exiled her from Hollywood for seven years
*Anastasia* (1956) marked her triumphant return to Hollywood after the scandal
Died on her 67th birthday, August 29, 1982, in London

items.prompt: Which Australian actress won Best Supporting Actress as Katharine Hepburn in *The Aviator* (2004) and Best Actress for *Blue Jasmine* (2013)?
items.explanation:
Won in both supporting and lead categories — one of the few actresses to achieve both
Her portrayal of Katharine Hepburn in *The Aviator* made her one of the rare actors to win an Oscar for playing another Oscar winner
*Blue Jasmine*: played a socialite in financial and psychological freefall, in a Woody Allen film inspired by *A Streetcar Named Desire*
Also acclaimed for playing Galadriel in *The Lord of the Rings* trilogy and the title role in *Elizabeth* (1998)
Co-artistic director of the Sydney Theatre Company from 2008 to 2013

items.prompt: Which actress won two Best Actress Oscars, for *Boys Don't Cry* (1999) and *Million Dollar Baby* (2004), both playing characters facing physical adversity?
items.explanation:
Won Best Actress twice within a five-year span — a rare feat for the category
*Boys Don't Cry*: played Brandon Teena, a transgender man murdered in Nebraska in 1993; Swank lived as a man for a month to prepare
*Million Dollar Baby*: played aspiring boxer Maggie Fitzgerald, trained by Clint Eastwood's character
Lived in a car with her mother as a teenager after moving to Los Angeles to pursue acting
The 11th actress in history to win two or more Best Actress awards
items.cardFront: 2 Best Actress wins: *Boys Don't Cry*, *Million Dollar Baby*

items.prompt: Which Australian actor won a posthumous Best Supporting Actor Oscar for playing the Joker in *The Dark Knight* (2008), having died of an accidental drug overdose at age 28?
items.explanation:
Died January 22, 2008, from an accidental overdose of prescription medications, six months before *The Dark Knight* premiered
Won Best Supporting Actor posthumously at the 81st Academy Awards (February 2009) — only the second posthumous acting winner, after Peter Finch (1976)
His Joker is widely regarded as one of the greatest villain performances in cinema history
Also acclaimed for *Brokeback Mountain* (2005), for which he received a Best Actor nomination
His Joker was inspired partly by punk rocker Sid Vicious and the character Alex from *A Clockwork Orange*
items.cardFront: Posthumous Supporting Actor: the Joker in *The Dark Knight*

links.text: Posthumous Supporting Actor: the Joker in *The Dark Knight*
